questions: Identify the primary U.S. federal law that governs the provision of special education and related services to children with disabilities. Within the regulations of this specific statute, determine the 'umbrella' eligibility category under which students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) typically qualify, given that ADHD is not listed as a standalone disability category. Additionally, provide the full name of the written legal document that this law mandates schools to develop for each eligible child to outline their specific learning goals and services.

For the neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent patterns of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity, identify the primary United States federal statute that governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children. Unlike civil rights laws that primarily ensure access through accommodations, this specific legislation mandates a 'free appropriate public education' (FAPE) tailored to individual needs. Name this act, identify the specific disability category under which the described disorder is most commonly classified for eligibility within this statute, and specify the acronym for the written plan required for every eligible school-aged child.

Identify the United States federal legislation that mandates the provision of free appropriate public education to eligible children with disabilities and governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention and special education. Specifically, regarding the domain of Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), determine the specific 'disability category' under which students with ADHD most commonly qualify for services, as the statute does not list ADHD as a standalone category. Furthermore, name the legally binding written plan that must be developed for every public school child who is found eligible for special education under this law.

In the context of United States federal education policy for students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the specific legislation that mandates the provision of special education services through an Individualized Education Program (IEP). Do not name the law in your initial search, but identify it based on its distinction from civil rights statutes (like Section 504) and its requirement for specialized instruction. Once this statute is identified, determine the specific 'umbrella' disability category under which students with ADHD are most frequently eligible for services, as ADHD is not listed as a standalone category in the law's primary definitions. Finally, detail the specific criteria regarding 'alertness' and 'educational performance' that a student must meet to qualify under this category.

In the context of United States federal education statutes protecting students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), identify the specific law that mandates the creation of an Individualized Education Program (IEP) for eligible children whose condition adversely affects their educational performance. After identifying this legislation, specify the exact disability category under which ADHD is typically classified for eligibility purposes according to government guidelines for this act, and explain the statute's requirement regarding the 'Least Restrictive Environment' (LRE).

In the context of United States public education, students diagnosed with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) are typically afforded rights and services under two primary federal laws. Identify these two distinct statutes. For each law, specify the particular eligibility criteria a student must meet to qualify for support (specifically regarding how the condition affects them) and provide the official name of the written document or plan that schools are mandated to develop for qualifying students.

rounds.answer_a:
I'll search for information about U.S. federal special education law and ADHD eligibility categories.

<search_quality_reflection>
I need to find specific information about:
1. The primary U.S. federal law for special education
2. The eligibility category for ADHD students under this law
3. The name of the mandated written document for eligible children
</search_quality_reflection>

<search_quality_score>4</search_quality_score>

<search>
U.S. federal law special education disabilities IDEA
</search>

<search>
ADHD eligibility category IDEA special education "Other Health Impairment"
</search>

<search>
IEP Individualized Education Program IDEA requirements document
</search>

The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the primary U.S. federal law governing special education and related services for children with disabilities [1][2]. This comprehensive statute ensures that eligible children receive a free appropriate public education (FAPE) tailored to their individual needs [1].

Students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) typically qualify for special education services under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) category [2][3]. The IDEA regulations define OHI as having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including heightened alertness to environmental stimuli that results in limited alertness to the educational environment, due to chronic or acute health problems [3]. ADHD falls under this category because it affects a student's alertness and attention in the classroom setting, substantially impacting their educational performance [2][3].

The written legal document mandated by IDEA for each eligible child is the Individualized Education Program (IEP) [1][4]. The IEP is a comprehensive written statement developed by a team including educators, parents, and specialists that outlines the child's present levels of academic achievement, measurable annual goals, special education services to be provided, and how progress will be measured [4]. This legally binding document must be reviewed and updated at least annually to ensure it continues to meet the student's evolving educational needs [1][4].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
[2] CDC - ADHD and Special Education Services
[3] IDEA Section 300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Definition
[4] Understood.org - What Is an IEP?
rounds.answer_b:
The primary U.S. federal law governing special education and related services for children with disabilities is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [1]. Although ADHD is not a specifically listed disability category under IDEA, students with ADHD typically qualify for services under the “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) eligibility category, which encompasses conditions characterized by limited strength, vitality, or alertness due to chronic or acute health problems that adversely affect educational performance—such as ADHD [2]. The OHI category requires that a student’s condition results in limited alertness to the educational environment and is accompanied by a demonstrated educational need [3]. Under IDEA, every eligible child must have an Individualized Education Program (IEP), a legally binding document that outlines the student’s unique educational needs, annual goals, specific special education services, accommodations, and modifications the school will provide. The IEP is developed collaboratively by a team that includes educators, specialists, parents, and, when appropriate, the student [4]. This document is reviewed and updated at least annually to ensure that the services continue to meet the child’s evolving needs and to maintain compliance with IDEA requirements [5].

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). *Individuals with Disabilities Education Act*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2022). *Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/ohi/  
[3] National Dissemination Center for Children with Disabilities. (2011). *Fact Sheet on Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/wp-content/uploads/nichcy_FS11.pdf  
[4] Understood. (2023). *What Is an Individualized Education Program (IEP)?* https://www.understood.org/en/school-learning/special-education/ieps  
[5] Wrightslaw. (2022). *IDEA 2004: The IEP Process*. https://www.wrightslaw.com/info/iep.index.htm

<search>
IDEA Individuals with Disabilities Education Act FAPE neurodevelopmental disorders
</search>

<search>
ADHD classification IDEA disability category "Other Health Impairment" special education
</search>

<search>
IEP Individualized Education Program IDEA requirements acronym
</search>

The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the primary United States federal statute that governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children with disabilities [1][2]. This comprehensive law ensures that children with disabilities receive a free appropriate public education (FAPE) tailored to their individual educational needs, going beyond mere access to mandate specialized instruction and support services [1].

The neurodevelopmental disorder described—characterized by persistent patterns of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity—is Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). Under IDEA, ADHD is most commonly classified within the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) disability category for eligibility determination [2][3]. The federal regulations define OHI as "having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" due to chronic or acute health problems, which specifically includes ADHD [3]. This classification recognizes that ADHD's impact on attention and focus substantially affects a student's educational performance and requires specialized support [2].

The acronym for the written plan required for every eligible school-aged child under IDEA is IEP, which stands for Individualized Education Program [1][4]. The IEP is a legally binding document developed by a multidisciplinary team that includes parents, teachers, and specialists. It comprehensively outlines the child's current performance levels, measurable annual goals, specific special education and related services to be provided, and methods for tracking progress [4]. This document must be reviewed and revised at least annually to ensure it continues to address the student's evolving educational needs and maintains compliance with IDEA's requirements for providing FAPE [1][4].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
[2] CDC - ADHD and School: Special Education Services
[3] 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Definition
[4] Understood.org - Understanding IEPs
rounds.answer_b:
The primary United States federal statute that governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to eligible children is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [1]. Originally enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act and subsequently reauthorized and renamed, IDEA ensures that eligible children with disabilities receive a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) tailored to their unique needs through specially designed instruction and related services [2]. This law applies to children from birth through age 21 and mandates services that are individualized, evidence-based, and provided at no cost to families [3]. 

For children diagnosed with a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent patterns of inattention and/or hyperactivity-impulsivity—commonly known as Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)—eligibility for services under IDEA is typically determined under the “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) category [4]. OHI includes chronic or acute health conditions such as ADHD that result in limited strength, vitality, or alertness, adversely affecting educational performance [5]. To qualify under OHI, a student must demonstrate that their ADHD impacts their ability to learn and function in an educational setting, necessitating specialized support.

For every eligible school-aged child, IDEA mandates the development of an Individualized Education Program (IEP) [6]. The IEP is a legally binding document that outlines the child’s present levels of academic achievement and functional performance, measurable annual goals, specific special education and related services, accommodations, and modifications [7]. It is created and reviewed annually by a multidisciplinary team that includes parents, general and special education teachers, school representatives, and, when appropriate, the student [8].

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). *Individuals with Disabilities Education Act*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] IDEA Part B Regulations, 34 C.F.R. §300.1  
[3] National Center for Learning Disabilities. (2023). *What Is IDEA?* https://www.ncld.org/idea/  
[4] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2022). *Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/ohi/  
[5] Understood. (2023). *ADHD and the IDEA: What You Need to Know*. https://www.understood.org/en/school-learning/special-education/child-access-to-special-education/adhd-and-the-idea-explained  
[6] Wrightslaw. (2022). *The IEP: A Legal Overview*. https://www.wrightslaw.com/iepwright/index.iep.htm  
[7] Council for Exceptional Children. (2021). *The IEP Process*. https://exceptionalchildren.org/  
[8] U.S. Department of Education. (2017). *A Guide to the Individualized Education Program*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/parents/iep/

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]:  [Wide Logic - Target 2]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
rounds.answer_a:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the United States federal legislation that mandates the provision of free appropriate public education (FAPE) to eligible children with disabilities [1]. Originally enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act and later renamed IDEA in 1990, this comprehensive statute governs how states and public agencies provide early intervention, special education, and related services to children from birth through age 21 [1][2].

IDEA establishes thirteen specific disability categories under which children may qualify for special education services. Since Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is not listed as a standalone disability category within IDEA's framework, students with ADHD most commonly qualify for services under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) category [2][3]. The federal regulations at 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) define Other Health Impairment as "having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" [3]. This definition specifically encompasses chronic or acute health problems such as ADHD, recognizing that the disorder's impact on attention, focus, and executive functioning can adversely affect a child's educational performance [2][3].

The legally binding written plan that must be developed for every public school child found eligible for special education under IDEA is the Individualized Education Program (IEP) [1][4]. The IEP serves as a comprehensive roadmap for the child's education, detailing present levels of academic achievement and functional performance, measurable annual goals, specific special education and related services to be provided, and methods for measuring progress [4]. Federal law requires that the IEP be developed by a multidisciplinary team including parents, general and special education teachers, school administrators, and relevant specialists [1][4]. This document must be reviewed at least annually and revised as necessary to ensure it continues to meet the student's evolving educational needs while maintaining compliance with IDEA's mandate to provide FAPE in the least restrictive environment [1][4].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
[2] CDC - ADHD and Special Education Services Under IDEA
[3] 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Federal Definition
[4] IDEA Section 614(d) - Individualized Education Programs
rounds.answer_b:
The primary United States federal legislation that mandates the provision of a free appropriate public education (FAPE) to eligible children with disabilities and governs how states and public agencies deliver early intervention, special education, and related services is the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [1]. Originally enacted in 1975 and most recently reauthorized in 2004, IDEA ensures that children with disabilities from birth through age 21 receive specialized instruction and support tailored to their unique needs, at no cost to families [2]. The law requires that services are delivered in the least restrictive environment and are based on individualized assessments and planning [3].

For students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), which is not listed as a standalone disability category under IDEA, eligibility for special education services is most commonly determined under the “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) category [4]. OHI is defined as having limited strength, vitality, or alertness due to chronic or acute health problems, such as ADHD, that adversely affect a child’s educational performance [5]. To qualify under OHI, a student must exhibit diminished alertness to the educational environment and demonstrate that the condition impacts their ability to learn, requiring specialized instruction or related services [6].

For every school-aged child found eligible under IDEA, a legally binding document known as the Individualized Education Program (IEP) must be developed [7]. The IEP outlines the student’s present levels of academic achievement and functional performance, sets measurable annual goals, specifies the special education services, accommodations, modifications, and supports the school will provide, and includes transition planning when appropriate [8]. The IEP is developed collaboratively by a team that includes parents, general and special education teachers, a school system representative, evaluation specialists, and, when relevant, the student [9]. This plan is reviewed and revised at least annually to ensure it remains aligned with the student’s evolving educational needs.

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). *Individuals with Disabilities Education Act*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] IDEA Part B Regulations, 34 C.F.R. §300.1  
[3] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2023). *IDEA Overview*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/idea/  
[4] Understood. (2023). *ADHD and the IDEA: What You Need to Know*. https://www.understood.org/en/school-learning/special-education/child-access-to-special-education/adhd-and-the-idea-explained  
[5] National Dissemination Center for Children with Disabilities. (2011). *Fact Sheet on Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/wp-content/uploads/nichcy_FS11.pdf  
[6] Child Mind Institute. (2023). *How ADHD Qualifies for Special Education*. https://childmind.org  
[7] Council for Exceptional Children. (2021). *The IEP Process*. https://exceptionalchildren.org/  
[8] Wrightslaw. (2022). *The IEP: A Legal Overview*. https://www.wrightslaw.com/iepwright/index.iep.htm  
[9] U.S. Department of Education. (2017). *A Guide to the Individualized Education Program*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/parents/iep/

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [Wide Logic - Target 2]:
rounds.answer_a:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the specific federal legislation that mandates the provision of special education services through an Individualized Education Program (IEP) for students with disabilities, including those with ADHD [1]. Unlike Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, which is a civil rights statute focused on preventing discrimination and providing accommodations, IDEA goes beyond ensuring access by requiring specialized instruction and related services tailored to each child's unique educational needs [1][2]. IDEA establishes a comprehensive framework for special education delivery, mandating that eligible students receive a free appropriate public education (FAPE) designed to meet their individual requirements [1].

Students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder most frequently qualify for services under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) disability category, as ADHD is not listed as a standalone category within IDEA's thirteen primary disability classifications [2][3]. The federal regulations at 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) specifically define Other Health Impairment to include ADHD among the chronic or acute health problems that can qualify a student for special education services [3].

To qualify under the Other Health Impairment category, students must meet specific criteria regarding alertness and educational performance. The regulatory definition states that OHI involves "having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" [3][4]. This heightened alertness to environmental stimuli is particularly relevant for students with ADHD, who may be easily distracted by extraneous sounds, movements, or visual stimuli in the classroom, thereby experiencing limited alertness to academic instruction [4].

The educational performance criterion requires that the health condition, including ADHD, "adversely affects a child's educational performance" [3][4]. This adverse effect must be demonstrated through comprehensive evaluation showing that the student's ADHD symptoms significantly impair their ability to benefit from general education instruction without specialized support [2][4]. The impact must be substantial enough to necessitate specially designed instruction, not merely accommodations, distinguishing IDEA eligibility from Section 504 eligibility [2]. Educational performance encompasses not only academic achievement but also functional performance in areas such as organization, task completion, social interactions, and behavioral regulation within the school environment [4].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
[2] Understood.org - IDEA vs. Section 504: What's the Difference?
[3] 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Definition
[4] Center for Parent Information and Resources - Other Health Impairment Fact Sheet
rounds.answer_b:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the federal statute that mandates the provision of special education services for eligible students with disabilities, including those with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), through the development of an Individualized Education Program (IEP) [1]. Unlike civil rights laws such as Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, which focus on non-discrimination and reasonable accommodations, IDEA requires the delivery of specialized instruction and related services designed to meet a child’s unique educational needs [2]. It guarantees a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) in the least restrictive environment and applies to children from birth through age 21 [3].

Although ADHD is not listed as a distinct category in IDEA, students with ADHD most frequently qualify for services under the “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) eligibility category [4]. OHI is defined as having limited strength, vitality, or alertness due to chronic or acute health problems that adversely affect a student’s educational performance [5]. According to IDEA regulations, to qualify under OHI, a student must demonstrate that their condition results in “limited alertness, including limited strength, vitality, or alertness (including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli) that is due to chronic or acute health problems such as ADHD” [6]. Furthermore, the impairment must adversely affect educational performance and require special education and related services [7]. This means that not only must the student have a medical or psychological diagnosis of ADHD, but the condition must also demonstrably interfere with their ability to learn or engage in the educational environment [8]. School-based evaluations must confirm that the student's attentional difficulties significantly impact academic achievement, social functioning, or behavioral performance in school settings [9]. Because OHI encompasses various health-related conditions that impact school functioning, it serves as the primary pathway for students with ADHD to access individually designed instruction and support through an IEP, rather than a less comprehensive 504 Plan [10].

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). *Individuals with Disabilities Education Act*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] IDEA Part B Regulations, 34 C.F.R. §300.34  
[3] National Center for Learning Disabilities. (2023). *What Is IDEA?* https://www.ncld.org/idea/  
[4] Understood. (2023). *ADHD and the IDEA: What You Need to Know*. https://www.understood.org/en/school-learning/special-education/child-access-to-special-education/adhd-and-the-idea-explained  
[5] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2022). *Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/ohi/  
[6] 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9)  
[7] Wrightslaw. (2022). *Other Health Impairment (OHI) Under IDEA*. https://www.wrightslaw.com/law/idea/ohi.other.health.impairment.html  
[8] Child Mind Institute. (2023). *How ADHD Qualifies for Special Education*. https://childmind.org/article/adhd-and-special-education/  
[9] Council for Exceptional Children. (2021). *The IEP Process*. https://exceptionalchildren.org/  
[10] U.S. Department of Education. (2017). *A Guide to the Individualized Education Program*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/parents/iep/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the legislation (IDEA) and the category (Other Health Impairment). However, Agent A provided a superior user experience through better structure and precision. 

1. **Structure**: The prompt asked to detail specific criteria regarding 'alertness' and 'educational performance'. Agent A dedicated specific paragraphs to each of these criteria, making the information easy to scan and digest. Agent B combined the category identification, definitions, and criteria into one dense paragraph.
2. **Precision**: Agent A provided an exact and clear quote of the regulatory definition for OHI. Agent B provided a slightly jumbled quote ("limited alertness, including limited strength... or alertness") that mixed the cause and effect clauses of the regulation.
3. **Completeness**: Agent A explicitly noted that 'educational performance' includes functional performance (social/behavioral), which is a critical detail for ADHD eligibility often missed when focusing solely on academics.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]:  [Wide Logic - Target 2]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
rounds.answer_a:
I'll search for information about U.S. federal education law, ADHD classification, and LRE requirements.

<search>
IDEA Individuals with Disabilities Education Act IEP requirements ADHD
</search>

<search>
ADHD Other Health Impairment category IDEA federal regulations
</search>

<search>
Least Restrictive Environment LRE IDEA requirements special education
</search>

The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the specific United States federal education statute that mandates the creation of an Individualized Education Program (IEP) for eligible children with disabilities, including those with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder whose condition adversely affects their educational performance [1][2]. This comprehensive federal law, originally enacted in 1975 as the Education for All Handicapped Children Act and renamed IDEA in 1990, establishes the legal framework requiring public schools to provide free appropriate public education (FAPE) to children with disabilities through specially designed instruction and related services [1].

According to federal government guidelines and regulations implementing IDEA, ADHD is typically classified under the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) disability category for eligibility purposes [2][3]. The Code of Federal Regulations at 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) specifically defines Other Health Impairment as "having limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" due to chronic or acute health problems, which explicitly includes attention deficit disorder or attention deficit hyperactivity disorder [3]. This classification recognizes that students with ADHD experience heightened distractibility to environmental stimuli that interferes with their ability to maintain focus on educational activities, thereby requiring specialized educational support [2][3].

IDEA's Least Restrictive Environment (LRE) requirement mandates that children with disabilities, including those with ADHD qualifying under OHI, must be educated with non-disabled peers to the maximum extent appropriate [4][5]. The statute specifically states that removal from the regular educational environment should occur only when the nature or severity of the disability prevents satisfactory education in regular classes, even with supplementary aids and services [4]. This means schools must first consider placement in general education classrooms with appropriate supports before considering more restrictive settings [5]. The LRE provision ensures that students with ADHD receive their IEP services in settings that promote interaction with typical peers while still meeting their individual educational needs, reflecting IDEA's commitment to inclusion and normalization of the educational experience for students with disabilities [4][5].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
[2] CDC - ADHD and Special Education Services
[3] 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Definition
[4] IDEA Section 612(a)(5) - Least Restrictive Environment Requirements
[5] OSERS Policy Guidance on Least Restrictive Environment
rounds.answer_b:
The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) is the primary federal law that mandates the creation of an Individualized Education Program (IEP) for eligible children with disabilities, including those with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD), when their condition adversely affects educational performance [1]. Unlike civil rights statutes such as Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act, which ensures access through accommodations, IDEA requires specially designed instruction and comprehensive services to ensure a Free Appropriate Public Education (FAPE) [2]. Although ADHD is not listed as a standalone disability category under IDEA, students with ADHD most commonly qualify for services under the “Other Health Impairment” (OHI) category [3]. According to the U.S. Department of Education, OHI includes conditions where there is “limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, due to chronic or acute health problems such as ADHD,” which results in adverse effects on educational performance [4]. To qualify, a student must demonstrate through evaluation that ADHD impacts their ability to learn, focus, or function in the school setting, necessitating special education support [5].

A core requirement of IDEA is the provision of services in the Least Restrictive Environment (LRE), meaning students with disabilities must be educated with their non-disabled peers to the maximum extent appropriate [6]. This mandate requires that removal from general education classrooms should only occur if the nature or severity of the disability prevents satisfactory achievement in regular classes, even with supplementary aids and services [7]. Placement decisions are determined by the IEP team and must be based on individual needs, ensuring that students are not unnecessarily segregated [8]. The LRE provision underscores IDEA’s commitment to inclusion, academic integration, and social development, reinforcing that students with disabilities, including those with ADHD, are entitled to participate fully in the general education environment unless otherwise justified by their educational needs [9].

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). *Individuals with Disabilities Education Act*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] IDEA Part B Regulations, 34 C.F.R. §300.34  
[3] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2022). *Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/ohi/  
[4] 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9)  
[5] Understood. (2023). *ADHD and the IDEA: What You Need to Know*. https://www.understood.org/en/school-learning/special-education/child-access-to-special-education/adhd-and-the-idea-explained  
[6] Wrightslaw. (2022). *Least Restrictive Environment (LRE)*. https://www.wrightslaw.com/law/idea/lre.index.html  
[7] National Center for Learning Disabilities. (2023). *What Is IDEA?* https://www.ncld.org/idea/  
[8] Council for Exceptional Children. (2021). *The IEP Process*. https://exceptionalchildren.org/  
[9] U.S. Department of Education. (2017). *A Guide to the Individualized Education Program*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/parents/iep/

Second, Agent B had much better formatting for its references (APA style with links/dates vs. Agent A's simple titles). rounds.question: In the context of United States public education, students diagnosed with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) are typically afforded rights and services under two primary federal laws. Identify these two distinct statutes. For each law, specify the particular eligibility criteria a student must meet to qualify for support (specifically regarding how the condition affects them) and provide the official name of the written document or plan that schools are mandated to develop for qualifying students.
checklist_width: Eligibility for Entity 1 (IDEA): Must fall under 'Other Health Impairment' and/or adversely affect educational performance
Document for Entity 1 (IDEA): Individualized Education Program (IEP)
Eligibility for Entity 2 (Section 504): Must have a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities (learning)
checklist_depth: Target Entity 1: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
Target Entity 2: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Reasoning by describing the legal category (federal laws protecting ADHD students) without naming the specific acts. It requires Wide Aggregation by forcing the comparison of two distinct frameworks (IDEA and Section 504) regarding specific attributes (eligibility nuances and document names), ensuring the agent does not conflate the two common standards found in the 'What You Need to Know' domain.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]:  [Wide Logic - Target 1]: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) [Wide Logic - Target 2]:
rounds.answer_a:
I'll search for information about the two federal laws that provide rights and services to students with ADHD in U.S. public education.

<search>
IDEA Section 504 ADHD eligibility criteria federal laws comparison
</search>

<search>
Section 504 plan versus IEP ADHD students eligibility requirements
</search>

<search>
IDEA Other Health Impairment ADHD educational performance criteria
</search>

<search>
Section 504 Rehabilitation Act substantial limitation major life activity ADHD
</search>

Students diagnosed with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in United States public education are afforded rights and services under two primary federal laws: the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973 [1][2].

The Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) provides comprehensive special education services to eligible students with disabilities [1]. For students with ADHD to qualify under IDEA, they must meet specific eligibility criteria within the "Other Health Impairment" (OHI) category, since ADHD is not listed as a standalone disability category [2][3]. The federal regulations define OHI as having "limited strength, vitality, or alertness, including a heightened alertness to environmental stimuli, that results in limited alertness with respect to the educational environment" [3]. Critically, the student's ADHD must adversely affect their educational performance to a degree that requires specially designed instruction, not merely accommodations [2][3]. For qualifying students, schools are mandated to develop an Individualized Education Program (IEP), a legally binding document that outlines present performance levels, measurable annual goals, specific special education services, and progress monitoring methods [1][4].

Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act provides broader protections as a civil rights statute prohibiting discrimination based on disability [2][5]. To qualify under Section 504, a student with ADHD must have a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities, which include learning, reading, concentrating, thinking, or communicating [5][6]. The threshold for Section 504 eligibility is generally lower than IDEA, as students need not require specialized instruction but merely need the condition to substantially limit a major life activity [2][5]. For eligible students, schools must develop a Section 504 Plan (sometimes called a 504 Accommodation Plan), which documents the specific accommodations, modifications, and services necessary to ensure the student has equal access to education [5][6].

The key distinction lies in their scope: IDEA mandates specialized instruction through an IEP for students whose ADHD significantly impairs educational performance, while Section 504 ensures equal access through a 504 Plan for students whose ADHD substantially limits major life activities, regardless of whether they need special education [2][5].

## References

[1] U.S. Department of Education - Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA)
[2] Understood.org - IDEA vs. Section 504: What's the Difference?
[3] 34 CFR §300.8(c)(9) - Other Health Impairment Definition
[4] IDEA Section 614(d) - Individualized Education Programs
[5] U.S. Department of Education - Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act
[6] OCR - Protecting Students with Disabilities: Section 504 FAQ
rounds.answer_b:
Students with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in U.S. public education are primarily protected under two federal laws: the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) and Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973. Under IDEA, a student must have a disability that falls within one of 13 specified categories and requires special education services to benefit from education [1]. ADHD is not a standalone category but typically qualifies under “Other Health Impairment” (OHI), defined as limited strength, vitality, or alertness due to chronic or acute health problems like ADHD that adversely affect educational performance [2]. To qualify, evaluations must show that the disorder impacts academic learning or social functioning to a degree requiring specially designed instruction [3]. For eligible students, schools must develop an Individualized Education Program (IEP), a legally binding document outlining specialized instruction, goals, accommodations, and services [4].

In contrast, Section 504 is a civil rights law prohibiting discrimination against individuals with disabilities in programs receiving federal funds [5]. Eligibility requires a physical or mental impairment that substantially limits one or more major life activities—including learning, concentrating, or thinking [6]. Because Section 504 has a broader definition of disability, students with ADHD who do not qualify under IDEA may still be eligible if their condition significantly impairs major life functions [7]. Unlike IDEA, Section 504 does not require specially designed instruction but mandates equal access through reasonable accommodations. For qualifying students, schools must create a Section 504 Plan, which documents the student’s needs and lists specific accommodations, such as extended time on tests or preferential seating, to ensure equal opportunity [8]. While an IEP under IDEA focuses on individualized, intensive educational programming with strict procedural safeguards, a Section 504 Plan emphasizes accessibility and inclusion through modifications within the general education setting [9].

## References  
[1] U.S. Department of Education. (2023). *Individuals with Disabilities Education Act*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/  
[2] 34 C.F.R. §300.8(c)(9)  
[3] Center for Parent Information and Resources. (2022). *Other Health Impairment*. https://www.parentcenterhub.org/ohi/  
[4] U.S. Department of Education. (2017). *A Guide to the Individualized Education Program*. https://sites.ed.gov/idea/parents/iep/  
[5] U.S. Department of Education, Office for Civil Rights. (2023). *Resources on Section 504*. https://www2.ed.gov/about/offices/list/ocr/504faq.html  
[6] Section 504, Rehabilitation Act of 1973, 29 U.S.C. §794  
[7] Understood. (2023). *ADHD and Section 504 Plans*. https://www.understood.org/en/school-learning/special-education/your-childs-rights/section-504-plans-and-adhd  
[8] Wrightslaw. (2022). *Section 504: Overview and Comparison to IDEA*. https://www.wrightslaw.com/law/504/  
[9] Child Mind Institute. (2023). *504 Plans and IEPs: What’s the Difference?* https://childmind.org/article/504-plans-vs-ieps-whats-the-difference/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents provided accurate, comprehensive answers that satisfied the checklist (identifying IDEA/OHI/IEP and Section 504/Substantial Limitation/504 Plan). However, Agent A included raw search tool logs and internal monologue ('I'll search for...') in the final output, which clutters the user experience. Agent B provided a clean, direct response with significantly better-formatted references (including URLs). While both agents failed to use bolding or bullet points to improve scannability, Agent B is the clear winner for presentation quality.
